section.title: Comfort for God's People
section.sectionRationale: This chapter opens the Book of Comfort, chapters 40 to 55, turning from the Hezekiah narratives and the exile they foretold toward a sustained message of hope. God commands His messengers to comfort His people, announces a voice preparing His way, and answers Israel's despair with an unmatched vision of His own incomparable power, wisdom, and tenderness as their Creator and Shepherd.
section.thesis: To a people who feel forgotten in exile, God speaks comfort by declaring their sin pardoned, sending a herald to prepare His way, and revealing Himself as the incomparable Creator who measures the oceans in His palm and yet gently carries His lambs, so that those who wait on Him will find their strength renewed.
section.frameNote: Nine movements: the command to comfort God's people; a voice preparing the way in the wilderness; the withering of all flesh against the enduring word of God; the LORD coming as tender Shepherd; the incomparable Creator who measures the world in His hand; the folly of comparing God to an idol; God enthroned above the nations; the Holy One with no equal; and the climactic promise that those who wait on the LORD will mount up like eagles.

verses.text: “{{nacham|Comfort}}, comfort My people,” says your God.
“Speak tenderly to Jerusalem, and proclaim to her that her forced labor has been completed; her iniquity has been pardoned. For she has received from the hand of the LORD double for all her sins.”

units.label: A voice in the wilderness
units.note: prepare the way of the LORD

verses.text: A voice of one calling: “Prepare the way for the LORD in the wilderness; make a straight highway for our God in the desert.
Every valley shall be lifted up, and every mountain and hill made low; the uneven ground will become smooth, and the rugged land a plain.
And the glory of the LORD will be revealed, and all humanity together will see it. For the mouth of the LORD has spoken.”

units.label: All flesh is grass
units.note: the word of our God stands forever

verses.text: A voice says, “Cry out!” And I asked, “What should I cry out?” “All flesh is like grass, and all its glory like the flowers of the field.
The grass withers and the flowers fall when the breath of the LORD blows on them; indeed, the people are grass.
The grass withers and the flowers fall, but the word of our God stands forever.”
Go up on a high mountain, O Zion, herald of good news. Raise your voice loudly, O Jerusalem, herald of good news. Lift it up, do not be afraid! Say to the cities of Judah, “Here is your God!”
Behold, the Lord GOD comes with might, and His arm establishes His rule. His reward is with Him, and His recompense accompanies Him.
He tends His flock like a shepherd; He gathers the lambs in His arms and carries them close to His heart. He gently leads the nursing ewes.

units.label: Who has measured the waters in His hand

verses.text: Who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand, or marked off the heavens with the span of his hand? Who has held the dust of the earth in a basket, or weighed the mountains on a scale and the hills with a balance?
Who has directed the Spirit of the LORD, or informed Him as His counselor?
Whom did He consult to enlighten Him, and who taught Him the paths of justice? Who imparted knowledge to Him and showed Him the way of understanding?
Surely the nations are like a drop in a bucket; they are considered a speck of dust on the scales; He lifts up the islands like fine dust.
Lebanon is not sufficient for fuel, nor its animals enough for a burnt offering.
All the nations are as nothing before Him; He regards them as nothingness and emptiness.

units.label: To whom will you liken God?

verses.text: To whom will you liken God? To what image will you compare Him?
To an idol that a craftsman casts and a metalworker overlays with gold and fits with silver chains?
To one bereft of an offering who chooses wood that will not rot, who seeks a skilled craftsman to set up an idol that will not topple?

units.label: He sits enthroned above the circle of the earth

verses.text: Do you not know? Have you not heard? Has it not been declared to you from the beginning? Have you not understood since the foundation of the earth?
He sits enthroned above the circle of the earth; its dwellers are like grasshoppers. He stretches out the heavens like a curtain, and spreads them out like a tent to dwell in.
He brings the princes to nothing and makes the rulers of the earth meaningless.
No sooner are they planted, no sooner are they sown, no sooner have their stems taken root in the ground, than He blows on them and they wither, and a whirlwind sweeps them away like stubble.

units.label: The Holy One has no equal

verses.text: “To whom will you liken Me, or who is My equal?” asks the {{qadosh|Holy One}}.
Lift up your eyes on high: Who created all these? He leads forth the starry host by number; He calls each one by name. Because of His great power and mighty strength, not one of them is missing.

units.label: Those who wait on the LORD
units.note: they will mount up like eagles

verses.text: Why do you say, O Jacob, and why do you assert, O Israel, “My way is hidden from the LORD, and my claim is ignored by my God”?
Do you not know? Have you not heard? The LORD is the everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth. He will not grow tired or weary; His understanding is beyond searching out.
He gives power to the faint and increases the strength of the weak.
Even youths grow tired and weary, and young men stumble and fall.
But those who {{qavah|wait}} upon the LORD will renew their strength; they will mount up with wings like eagles; they will run and not grow weary, they will walk and not faint.

nacham.gloss: To comfort, console, or bring relief, sometimes carrying the sense of a change that brings ease after grief. The double command, comfort, comfort, opens the whole second half of Isaiah with tenderness rather than further rebuke, announcing that the season of God's people receiving punishment for their sin is over and the season of consolation has begun.

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart, utterly other. Isaiah's favorite title for God, the Holy One, appears here at the climax of the chapter's argument for God's incomparability: no idol, no ruler, no created thing can be likened to Him, because He alone is qadosh in being and power.

qavah.gloss: To wait, to hope, to look eagerly for, often with the sense of a rope stretched taut in expectation. Those who qavah upon the LORD are not passively idle but actively, hopefully straining toward Him, and the chapter promises that this kind of waiting is rewarded with renewed strength beyond human capacity.

basic.context: After the exile foretold in chapter 39, Isaiah's book turns a corner into pure comfort. God commands His messengers to speak tenderly to Jerusalem, announces a voice that will prepare His way, and answers a weary, doubting people with an overwhelming vision of His own incomparable greatness: He measures oceans in His palm, He knows every star by name, and yet He carries His people like a shepherd carries lambs.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter first spoke to Judah in or facing exile, promising that their punishment was complete and comfort was coming. Its third movement, verses 3 to 5, is directly applied in all four Gospels to John the Baptist preparing the way for Jesus, showing that Isaiah's comfort ultimately arrives in a person, not merely a historical return from Babylon. The chapter's vision of God's incomparable power is meant to answer real despair, not merely to impress with poetry.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with {{nacham}}, comfort, doubled for emphasis, marking a decisive turn from judgment to tenderness. It closes by naming God the {{qadosh}}, the Holy One, utterly incomparable to any idol or ruler, and promising that those who {{qavah}}, wait or hope, upon Him will find strength beyond their own resources.
basic.typology: The voice preparing a straight highway in the wilderness for the LORD's coming, verses 3 to 5, is fulfilled in John the Baptist's ministry preparing the way for Jesus, cited in Matthew 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4-6, and John 1:23. The tender Shepherd who gathers lambs in His arms, verse 11, anticipates Christ, the Good Shepherd, John 10:11.
basic.christ: All four Gospels apply verses 3 to 5 directly to John the Baptist, the voice crying in the wilderness who prepared the way for Jesus, the LORD whose glory would be revealed for all humanity to see, Matthew 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4-6, John 1:23. The Shepherd of verse 11, who carries lambs close to His heart, is the same Shepherd Jesus names Himself in John 10:11, laying down His life for the sheep. The everlasting, never-weary Creator of verse 28 is revealed in John 1:1-3 as the Word who became flesh.
basic.god: God comforts His people not by minimizing what they have suffered but by revealing who He truly is: the Creator whose power dwarfs every nation and every idol, and yet the Shepherd whose tenderness carries the weakest lamb. He never grows weary and His understanding cannot be exhausted, so those who wait on Him, however exhausted themselves, will find strength that is not their own.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4-6, and John 1:23 all apply verses 3 to 5 to John the Baptist preparing the way for Christ. John 10:11 identifies Jesus as the Good Shepherd. Psalm 103:15-16 echoes the image of human life as grass that withers. Habakkuk 3:19 shares the imagery of strength like a deer on the heights.
basic.oneStory: This chapter turns the whole story of Isaiah, and in a sense the whole Bible, from judgment toward comfort: a weary, exiled people are told their punishment is complete and their God is coming. That coming is fulfilled when John the Baptist prepares the way and Jesus Himself arrives as the incomparable Creator and tender Shepherd, in whom weary people find their strength renewed.

context.literary: A carefully structured hymn of comfort in nine movements, moving from a tender command, through a herald's cry, a meditation on human frailty against God's enduring word, a vision of the Shepherd-King, an extended argument for God's incomparable power and wisdom, a mockery of idols, and closing with the promise of renewed strength for those who wait on Him.
context.historical: Opens the Book of Comfort, chapters 40 to 55, addressed to a people facing or already experiencing the Babylonian exile that chapter 39 foretold, offering hope of pardon and return grounded not in political circumstance but in the character of God Himself.
context.canonical: The hinge between the Hezekiah narratives and the sustained hope of Isaiah's second half; its opening words, 'comfort, comfort my people,' give the whole section its traditional name, and its highway imagery is directly cited by all four Gospels regarding John the Baptist.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To turn a weary, exiled people from despair to hope by announcing that their sin has been dealt with, that God Himself is coming to them, and that His incomparable power and tender care together guarantee their strength will be renewed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's comfort for a specific historical crisis and promises a coming deliverance; it is not a formula guaranteeing immediate relief from every hardship. Its abiding principle is that comfort comes from knowing who God truly is, and that waiting on Him renews strength beyond human capacity, a pattern the New Testament shows fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: What does 'double for all her sins,' verse 2, mean, an exact repayment, or something else?

views.label: An idiom for complete, full payment
views.case: Hebrew idiom often uses 'double' to mean ample or complete measure rather than a literal mathematical doubling, so the phrase announces that Jerusalem's punishment is fully and completely finished, not that God exacted more than was just.

views.label: A literal doubling that raises questions about divine justice
views.case: Some readers take the phrase more literally, as an intensified punishment, and see it addressed by later Scripture's insistence that God's judgments are always measured and just, never excessive.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees on the chapter's main point: Jerusalem's time of judgment is fully over, and the season of comfort has now decisively begun.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's opening command is {{nacham}}, comfort, doubled to mark a real turning point after long chapters of judgment. Its argument for God's incomparability climaxes in the title {{qadosh}}, the Holy One, utterly set apart from every idol and ruler, and it closes with the promise that those who {{qavah}}, wait or hope, upon Him will find strength that is not their own.

typology.type: The voice preparing a highway for the LORD in the wilderness
typology.fulfillment: John the Baptist's ministry preparing the way for Jesus, Matthew 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4-6, John 1:23.

typology.type: The Shepherd who gathers lambs in His arms
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, John 10:11.

study.christ: All four Gospels apply this chapter's central image, a voice preparing a straight highway in the wilderness, directly to John the Baptist, who prepared the way for Jesus, the LORD whose glory would be revealed to all humanity, Matthew 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4-6, John 1:23. The tender Shepherd of verse 11, gathering lambs and carrying them close to His heart, is the same Shepherd Jesus names Himself, laying down His life for His sheep, John 10:11. The everlasting Creator who never grows weary, verse 28, is revealed as the eternal Word who became flesh and dwelt among us, John 1:1-3, 14.
study.god: God comforts His weary people not by denying their suffering but by revealing His own incomparable greatness: the Creator who measures oceans in His palm and calls every star by name, and yet the Shepherd who gathers the weakest lamb close to His heart. He never grows tired, His understanding is beyond searching out, and He gives that same inexhaustible strength to those who wait on Him.

crossReferences.note: Applies Isaiah 40:3 directly to John the Baptist's ministry preparing the way for Jesus.
John the Baptist himself identifies as the voice of Isaiah 40:3 crying in the wilderness.
Jesus identifies Himself as the Good Shepherd, fulfilling the image of the Shepherd who gathers His lambs in Isaiah 40:11.
Echoes the imagery of human life as grass and flowers that quickly wither, set against God's enduring word.

study.oneStory: This chapter turns Isaiah's whole story, and in a real sense the whole Bible's story, from judgment toward comfort: a weary, exiled people are told their sin is dealt with and their God is coming to them. That coming is fulfilled when John the Baptist prepares the way and Jesus arrives as the incomparable Creator and tender Shepherd, in whom the weary find their strength made new.

sermon.title: Behold Your God!
sermon.why: Begg's four part series walks straight through Isaiah 40:1-31, the whole passage covered in this study, showing how God urges His people to respond to national calamity by casting their eyes afresh on Him as gentle Shepherd and holy, transcendent King.

quiz.q: What command opens this chapter in verses 1 to 2?

options.text: Comfort My people, for Jerusalem's iniquity has been pardoned.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 give exactly this command, marking the turn from judgment to comfort.

options.text: Judge My people more severely for their sins.
options.note: The opposite; the command is to comfort, since the punishment is declared complete.

options.text: Warn My people of even greater coming judgment.
options.note: Not the content of these verses, which announce pardon and comfort, not further warning.

options.text: Ignore My people until they repent further.
options.note: The opposite; God commands His messengers to speak tenderly, not to withhold comfort.

quiz.q: How do the Gospels apply the voice crying in the wilderness, verses 3 to 5?

options.text: To John the Baptist, who prepared the way for Jesus.
options.note: All four Gospels apply this passage directly to John the Baptist's ministry.

options.text: To the prophet Isaiah himself and no one else.
options.note: The Gospels apply it specifically to John the Baptist preparing the way for Christ, not to Isaiah's own ministry.

options.text: To a future angelic being with no human identity.
options.note: The Gospels identify a specific human figure, John the Baptist, not an angelic being.

options.text: To the Babylonian officials who allowed the exiles to return.
options.note: The New Testament's application is to John the Baptist and Christ, not to Babylonian officials.

quiz.q: What contrast does verses 6 to 8 draw between human life and God's word?

options.text: All flesh is like grass that withers, but the word of God stands forever.
options.note: Verses 6 to 8 state this contrast directly.

options.text: Both human life and God's word are equally temporary.
options.note: The opposite; the passage contrasts fleeting human life with God's enduring, permanent word.

options.text: Human life is eternal while God's word changes over time.
options.note: The passage reverses this: human life withers like grass, while God's word alone endures forever.

options.text: Only the wicked wither like grass, not the righteous.
options.note: The text applies this to 'all flesh' generally, not selectively to the wicked alone.

quiz.q: What image describes God's incomparable power in verse 12?

options.text: He has measured the waters in the hollow of His hand and weighed the mountains on a scale.
options.note: Verse 12 uses exactly this image of effortless, immense power.

options.text: He struggles to control the seas and mountains.
options.note: The opposite; the image conveys effortless mastery, not struggle.

options.text: He shares equal power with the sun and moon.
options.note: The passage presents God as uniquely incomparable, not sharing power with created things.

options.text: He created the earth but has since withdrawn from governing it.
options.note: The passage presents God as actively measuring and governing creation, not withdrawn from it.

quiz.q: What promise closes the chapter in verse 31?

options.text: Those who wait on the LORD will renew their strength and mount up with wings like eagles.
options.note: Verse 31 gives exactly this climactic promise of renewed strength for those who wait on God.

options.text: Only young, strong people will ever find renewed strength.
options.note: The opposite; the passage specifically contrasts even tired youths with those who wait on the LORD, who alone are renewed regardless of natural strength.

options.text: Waiting on the LORD guarantees the removal of all future hardship.
options.note: The promise concerns renewed strength and endurance, not the total absence of future hardship.

options.text: Strength comes only through human effort and training.
options.note: The opposite; the chapter contrasts human effort, which grows weary, with strength that comes specifically from waiting on the LORD.

questions: God's comfort begins by declaring that Jerusalem's sin has been dealt with. Where do you need to receive that same comfort, believing your sin is truly pardoned in Christ rather than still held against you?
John the Baptist prepared a straight highway for the LORD's coming. What obstacles, valleys, or crooked places in your own heart need to be leveled to make room for Christ this season?

All flesh is like grass, but God's word stands forever. What are you currently trusting in that is more fragile than you realize, and how could you anchor yourself more in God's enduring word instead?

God is pictured as both the Creator who measures oceans in His hand and the Shepherd who carries lambs close to His heart. Which of these truths, His greatness or His tenderness, do you need to remember most today?

The chapter promises that those who wait on the LORD will renew their strength. Where are you currently running on empty, and what would it look like this week to actually wait on Him rather than push forward in your own strength?

translationNotes.note: Double for all her sins is best understood as a Hebrew idiom for full, complete measure rather than a literal doubling of just punishment; the point is that Jerusalem's time of discipline is fully finished.
All four Gospels quote this verse and apply it to John the Baptist, Matthew 3:3, Mark 1:3, Luke 3:4-6, John 1:23, making it one of the most directly cited verses from Isaiah in the New Testament.
Wait renders the Hebrew qavah, which carries the sense of eager, hopeful expectation rather than passive delay, closer to straining forward in hope than simply marking time.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 40:1-31. No em or en dashes appeared in the source text for this passage.
